does: This paper introduces AMNESIA, a large-scale benchmark for evaluating machine unlearning in medical language models. The benchmark comprises 70,560 question-answer pairs derived from 8,820 patient notes across 11 disease categories, with both factual recall and clinical reasoning questions. The authors evaluate four existing unlearning methods at patient and disease levels, revealing that removing individual patient data degrades clinical knowledge shared across patients with the same condition. They also introduce a medical terminology leakage metric to detect whether unlearned content remains recoverable through indirect means, demonstrating a fundamental tension between patient privacy and clinical knowledge preservation.
connects: This work builds on growing literature in machine unlearning that seeks alternatives to full model retraining when removing training data influence. While prior unlearning research has focused on synthetic or general-domain benchmarks, this paper addresses a critical gap by bringing unlearning evaluation to medical domains where data removal is both legally required and clinically sensitive. The work extends evaluation frameworks from general NLP unlearning by incorporating domain-specific considerations like disease categories and clinical reasoning, while connecting to broader efforts in medical AI safety and privacy-preserving machine learning that have emphasized the need for careful evaluation in healthcare applications.
enables: This benchmark enables development of specialized unlearning methods that can preserve general clinical knowledge while removing patient-specific information, addressing a key challenge revealed by the findings. Future work can use AMNESIA to design techniques that better disentangle individual patient data from disease-level patterns and shared medical knowledge. The benchmark also supports research into privacy-utility tradeoffs in medical AI, potentially informing regulatory approaches to model updates and data removal in clinical settings. Beyond unlearning, AMNESIA provides infrastructure for studying knowledge organization in medical LLMs and could facilitate research on controlled forgetting that maintains clinical competence while respecting patient privacy.
